original_abstract:
Inspired by the recent synthesis of bipartite sodalite clathrates $X\text{B}_3\text{C}_3$ ($X = \text{Sr, La}$), we employed density functional theory (DFT) calculations to investigate this structural family, exploring configurations with up to two distinct metal guests. A DFT-chemical pressure analysis of the $X = \text{Mg, Ca, Sr, Ba}$ series demonstrates that the ionic radius of the metal guest is a decisive factor for stability; specifically, the metal size must be precisely tuned to stabilize the boron-carbon framework against ambient-pressure dynamic instabilities. 

By conducting high-throughput DFT screenings of 105 binary-guest compounds with $Pm\bar{3}$ symmetry ($XY\text{B}_6\text{C}_6$, where $X$ and $Y$ are electropositive metals), we identified 22 phases that remain dynamically stable at 1 atm. This discovery expands the library of potentially synthesizable clathrate phases by 19, including 18 new metallic conductors and one insulator. Our results show that the superconducting critical temperature ($T_c$) and the density of states at the Fermi level can be effectively modulated by adjusting the average oxidation state of the guest atoms, with $T_c$ reaching its peak at an average valence of +1.5. Among all studied $Pm\bar{3}n$ $X\text{B}_3\text{C}_3$ and $Pm\bar{3}$ $XY\text{B}_6\text{C}_6$ phases, $\text{KPbB}_6\text{C}_6$ stands out with a predicted ambient-pressure Eliashberg $T_c$ of 88 K. Thermodynamic calculations suggest that $\text{KPbB}_6\text{C}_6$ can be synthesized using high-pressure, high-temperature methods and successfully quenched to ambient conditions, offering a promising route toward high-temperature superconductivity in recoverable covalent materials.
humanized_abstract:
Following the recent synthesis of bipartite sodalite clathrates X B3 C3 (X = Sr, La), we investigated this structural family using density functional theory (DFT) calculations to study variations of the metal guest up to two different species embedded within the structure. A DFT-chemical pressure analysis of the X = Mg, Ca, Sr, Ba series showed that the ionic radius of the metal guest is a critical factor to determine the structural stability of these compounds. The size of the metal guest must be precisely adjusted to keep the B3 C3 cage structure stable against dynamic instabilities at ambient pressure.

High-throughput studies of phases stable under ambient pressure using Density Functional Theory We have made high-throughput Density Functional Theory studies of the stability of 105 binary-guest compounds with $Pm\bar{3}m$ $XY\text{B}_6\text{C}_6$ structure, in the form $XY\text{B}_6\text{C}_6$ where $X$ and $Y$ are electropositive elements. In the high pressure phase, $XY$ atoms take the places of $\text{C}_6$ or $\text{B}_6$ units which produce no additional energy penalty on replacing $X$ or $Y$ with $\text{C}_6$ or $\text{B}_6$ within XY sites. We have investigated stability of several such structures and identified 22 phases which are dynamically stable at 1 atm pressure which thus forms part of library of clathrate structures that can be synthesize in the laboratory. Further we have the largest collection of metallic conductors and insulators, an increase of 18 such elements, in addition to one insulator. Additionally we found that $T_c$ and density of states at the Fermi level can be controlled over a large range of values simply by changing the average oxidation state of guest elements which has a maximum at an average valence of 1.5. As the highest ambient pressure Eliashberg $T_c$ is predicted for $\text{KPbB}_6\text{C}_6$, we carry out thermodynamics studies on synthesis of this structure under high pressure, high temperature and quenching it to ambient pressure which appears to be possible, thereby giving possible example of high temperature superconductor material in recoverable covalent form.

document.output:

As the highest ambient pressure Eliashberg $T_c$ is predicted for $\text{KPbB}_6\text{C}_6$, we carry out thermodynamics studies on synthesis of this structure under high pressure, high temperature and quenching it to ambient pressure which appears to be possible, thereby giving possible example of high temperature superconductor material in recoverable covalent form.
document.input:
Inspired by the recent synthesis of bipartite sodalite clathrates $X\text{B}_3\text{C}_3$ ($X = \text{Sr, La}$), we employed density functional theory (DFT) calculations to investigate this structural family, exploring configurations with up to two distinct metal guests. A DFT-chemical pressure analysis of the $X = \text{Mg, Ca, Sr, Ba}$ series demonstrates that the ionic radius of the metal guest is a decisive factor for stability; specifically, the metal size must be precisely tuned to stabilize the boron-carbon framework against ambient-pressure dynamic instabilities. 

By conducting high-throughput DFT screenings of 105 binary-guest compounds with $Pm\bar{3}$ symmetry ($XY\text{B}_6\text{C}_6$, where $X$ and $Y$ are electropositive metals), we identified 22 phases that remain dynamically stable at 1 atm. This discovery expands the library of potentially synthesizable clathrate phases by 19, including 18 new metallic conductors and one insulator. Our results show that the superconducting critical temperature ($T_c$) and the density of states at the Fermi level can be effectively modulated by adjusting the average oxidation state of the guest atoms, with $T_c$ reaching its peak at an average valence of +1.5. Among all studied $Pm\bar{3}n$ $X\text{B}_3\text{C}_3$ and $Pm\bar{3}$ $XY\text{B}_6\text{C}_6$ phases, $\text{KPbB}_6\text{C}_6$ stands out with a predicted ambient-pressure Eliashberg $T_c$ of 88 K. Thermodynamic calculations suggest that $\text{KPbB}_6\text{C}_6$ can be synthesized using high-pressure, high-temperature methods and successfully quenched to ambient conditions, offering a promising route toward high-temperature superconductivity in recoverable covalent materials.
